section.title: Make a Joyful Noise to the Lord
section.sectionRationale: The shortest and best known of the enthronement psalms, this five-verse hymn of thanksgiving closes the sequence begun in Psalm 93 with a universal summons to joyful, grateful worship grounded in the LORD's identity as Maker and Shepherd and His goodness that endures to every generation.
section.thesis: Because the LORD is God, our Maker and Shepherd, and because He is good, His loving devotion and faithfulness enduring forever, all the earth is summoned to enter His presence with joyful songs and thanksgiving.
section.frameNote: Two cycles of command and reason: a call to joyful, grateful worship because the LORD made us and we are His, verses 1-3; and a call to thankful entry into His presence because He is good and everlastingly faithful, verses 4-5.

units.label: Know that the Lord is God
units.note: a call to joyful worship of our Maker

verses.text: A Psalm of thanksgiving. Make a joyful noise to the LORD, all the earth.
Serve the LORD with gladness; come into His presence with joyful songs.
{{yada|Know}} that the LORD is God. It is He who made us, and we are His; we are His people, and the sheep of His pasture.

units.label: His loving devotion endures forever
units.note: a call to thankful entry into His presence

verses.text: Enter His gates with thanksgiving and His courts with praise; give thanks to Him and bless His name.
For the LORD is good, and His {{chesed|loving devotion}} endures forever; His faithfulness continues to all generations.

yada.gloss: To know intimately, not merely intellectually. Knowing that the LORD is God grounds all true worship in settled fact rather than fleeting feeling, an intimate acknowledgment of who made us and to whom we belong.

chesed.gloss: Loving devotion, steadfast covenant love. This word anchors Israel's whole understanding of God's character, His love that does not depend on human performance but endures forever, fully revealed in Christ's own steadfast love for His people.

basic.context: This short psalm of thanksgiving gathers up the enthronement sequence of Psalms 93-99 into a five-verse invitation: know that the LORD is God, our Maker and Shepherd, and therefore enter His presence with joyful songs and grateful praise, because His goodness and faithfulness never run out.
basic.hermeneutics: The psalm's global address, 'all the earth,' functions as a genuine summons to every people, not poetic flourish, resting on the same truth Genesis 1 and Israel's covenant both affirm, that the God who made all people is worthy of worship from all people.
basic.originalLanguages: To yada, know, that the LORD is God is more than intellectual assent, it is intimate, settled acknowledgment of His identity as Maker and Shepherd. His chesed, loving devotion, is the steadfast, covenant love that endures forever and anchors the psalm's closing confidence.
basic.typology: The image of God's people as 'the sheep of His pasture' anticipates Jesus' own words, 'I am the good shepherd... I know My sheep and My sheep know Me,' John 10:11, 14, showing that the intimate care this psalm celebrates finds its fullest expression in Christ.
basic.christ: Jesus identifies Himself directly with this psalm's shepherd imagery when He declares, 'I am the good shepherd,' John 10:11, and the psalm's call for 'all the earth' to enter God's gates with thanksgiving anticipates the ingathering of every nation into worship of God through Christ, fulfilling the psalm's universal scope.
basic.god: God is Maker, the one who made us rather than we ourselves, and Shepherd, the one who cares intimately for His people as His own flock, and He is unfailingly good, His loving devotion and faithfulness reaching to every generation without exception.
basic.crossReferences: John 10:11-16 shows Jesus as the good Shepherd who knows His sheep, echoing this psalm's pastoral imagery. Psalm 95:7 shares the same 'sheep of His pasture' language. Hebrews 13:15 calls believers to offer a continual sacrifice of praise through Jesus. Revelation 7:9 pictures a multitude from every nation gathered in worship, fulfilling this psalm's universal summons.
basic.oneStory: This psalm's summons for all the earth to know the LORD as Maker and Shepherd and to enter His presence with grateful joy is fulfilled in Christ, the good Shepherd who knows His own, through whom people of every nation are gathered into everlasting thanksgiving before God.

context.literary: Structured in two brief cycles of command followed by reason, verses 1-2 grounded in verse 3, and verse 4 grounded in verse 5, this psalm's economy of language makes it one of the most memorable and most quoted calls to worship in the whole Psalter.
context.historical: Possibly used as pilgrims approached Jerusalem's temple courts, similar to the Songs of Ascent, this psalm may reflect either Davidic worship or the fresh gratitude of the post-exilic community restoring temple worship, Ezra 3:10-13.
context.canonical: Closes the sustained 'the LORD reigns' sequence of Psalms 93-100 with a note of pure, uncomplicated thanksgiving, functioning across church history, in liturgies such as the Jubilate Deo, as one of the most widely used calls to corporate worship.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To summon all the earth to enter God's presence with joyful, grateful worship, grounding that summons in the settled knowledge that the LORD alone is God, our Maker and Shepherd, whose goodness and faithfulness never fail.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm describes ancient Israel's temple worship, entering literal gates and courts with thanksgiving; it prescribes for every believer, in every place, the abiding pattern of grounding joyful worship in the settled truth of who God is rather than in changing circumstances or feelings.

debates.question: Does verse 3's phrase mean 'we are His' or, as some ancient manuscripts and a marginal reading suggest, 'and not we ourselves,' emphasizing that we did not make ourselves?

views.label: 'We are His,' emphasizing belonging
views.case: Most Hebrew manuscripts and the main line of translation read 'we are His,' fitting the psalm's larger emphasis on belonging to God as His people and His flock.

views.label: 'Not we ourselves,' emphasizing that God alone is Creator
views.case: A textual variant, reflected in some translations' footnotes, reads 'and not we ourselves,' stressing that human beings did not create or sustain their own existence, in contrast to any human pretension of self-creation.

debates.bearing: Both readings arrive at the same worshipful conclusion: our existence and identity as God's people rest entirely on His creative and shepherding action, not on anything we have done for ourselves.

study.originalLanguages: To yada, know, that the LORD is God is intimate acknowledgment, not bare information, of His identity as Maker and Shepherd. His chesed, loving devotion, the steadfast covenant love that never runs dry, grounds the psalm's confident, joyful summons to worship.

typology.type: God's people as 'the sheep of His pasture,' verse 3
typology.fulfillment: Jesus declares Himself the good Shepherd who knows His sheep and lays down His life for them, John 10:11-16, fulfilling this psalm's pastoral image in His own person.

typology.type: The universal summons for 'all the earth' to worship, verse 1
typology.fulfillment: Revelation 7:9 pictures a great multitude from every nation, tribe, and tongue gathered before God's throne in thanksgiving and praise.

study.christ: Jesus takes up this psalm's own pastoral language when He says, 'I am the good shepherd; I know My sheep and My sheep know Me,' John 10:14, showing that the intimate care and belonging this psalm celebrates between the LORD and His people is embodied fully in Christ. And the psalm's summons for 'all the earth' to enter God's gates with thanksgiving is fulfilled as believers from every nation are gathered into worship through Him, Hebrews 13:15.
study.god: God is Maker, the one who formed us, and He is Shepherd, the one who cares personally for His people as His own flock. He is fundamentally good, and His loving devotion and faithfulness are not seasonal or conditional but endure forever, reaching to every generation without exception.

crossReferences.note: Jesus identifies Himself as the good Shepherd who knows His sheep, fulfilling this psalm's pastoral imagery of God's intimate care.
Shares the same 'sheep of His pasture' language, forming a shared thread across the enthronement psalms.
Calls believers to offer a continual sacrifice of praise to God through Jesus, echoing this psalm's call to enter His gates with thanksgiving.
Pictures a vast multitude from every nation gathered in worship, fulfilling this psalm's summons for all the earth to praise God.

study.oneStory: This psalm's brief, joyful summons for all the earth to know the LORD as Maker and Shepherd and enter His presence with thanksgiving is fulfilled in Christ, the good Shepherd who knows His own and gathers people from every nation into the everlasting thanksgiving this psalm anticipates.

sermon.title: A Psalm for Giving Thanks
sermon.why: Preached directly on Psalm 100:1-5, as confirmed by the sermon's own scripture reference on truthforlife.org, Begg examines this psalm's call to joyful, thankful worship grounded in the truths of God's Word, showing that entering His gates with praise depends on the redeeming work of the Lord Jesus Christ.

quiz.q: Who is summoned to make a joyful noise to the LORD in verse 1?

options.text: All the earth.
options.note: The psalm's opening summons is explicitly universal in scope, addressed to all the earth.

options.text: Only the priests serving in the temple.
options.note: The summons reaches far beyond the priesthood to all the earth.

options.text: Only the tribe of Levi.
options.note: The call is universal, not limited to a single tribe.

options.text: Only the nation of Israel.
options.note: Israel is certainly included, but the psalm's address explicitly extends to all the earth.

quiz.q: What is the stated reason for worship in verse 3?

options.text: Know that the LORD is God; He made us, and we are His people, the sheep of His pasture.
options.note: Verse 3 grounds worship in the settled knowledge of God's identity as Maker and Shepherd.

options.text: Because Israel has just won a military victory.
options.note: The reason given is God's identity as Creator and Shepherd, not a military victory.

options.text: Because the harvest has been unusually abundant this year.
options.note: The stated reason is theological, God's identity, not agricultural abundance.

options.text: Because the psalmist wants to impress other nations.
options.note: The reason given is grounded in God's own character and identity, not a desire to impress others.

quiz.q: How does the psalm describe the manner of entering God's presence in verse 4?

options.text: With thanksgiving and praise.
options.note: Verse 4 specifically calls worshipers to enter His gates with thanksgiving and His courts with praise.

options.text: With silence and complete withdrawal from speech.
options.note: The psalm calls for thanksgiving and praise, not silence.

options.text: With elaborate sacrificial rituals exclusively.
options.note: The psalm names thanksgiving and praise, not a detailed sacrificial system, as the manner of entry.

options.text: With formal legal petitions only.
options.note: The psalm's language is of thanksgiving and praise, not legal petition.

quiz.q: According to verse 5, how long do the LORD's goodness and faithfulness last?

options.text: His loving devotion endures forever, and His faithfulness continues to all generations.
options.note: Verse 5 explicitly states these qualities endure forever and reach every generation.

options.text: Only for the lifetime of the current generation.
options.note: The text explicitly extends God's faithfulness to all generations, not just the current one.

options.text: Only until the next national crisis occurs.
options.note: The psalm presents God's faithfulness as unconditional and enduring, not contingent on the absence of crisis.

options.text: For a limited, specified number of centuries.
options.note: The psalm's language is of permanence, 'forever' and 'to all generations,' not a specified limited period.

quiz.q: How does Jesus take up this psalm's pastoral imagery in the New Testament?

options.text: He declares Himself the good Shepherd who knows His sheep, John 10:11-14.
options.note: Jesus directly applies the shepherd and sheep imagery of this psalm to His own relationship with His people.

options.text: He rejects the shepherd and sheep imagery as inadequate.
options.note: Jesus embraces and fulfills this imagery rather than rejecting it.

options.text: He applies it only to the twelve apostles and no one else.
options.note: Jesus' shepherd imagery in John 10 extends to all His sheep, not the twelve apostles exclusively.

options.text: He applies it exclusively to Israel's ancient kings.
options.note: Jesus applies the shepherd imagery to Himself personally, not merely to Israel's past kings.

questions: This psalm grounds joyful worship in the settled knowledge that 'the LORD is God' rather than in shifting feelings or circumstances. What would change in your own worship this week if it were anchored more firmly in who God is rather than in how you feel?
The psalm calls God's people 'the sheep of His pasture,' an image of complete dependence and care. Where in your life do you resist depending on God as fully as a sheep depends on its shepherd?
Entering God's gates is to be done 'with thanksgiving,' not grudgingly or out of mere obligation. What specific things could you name right now as reasons for genuine thanksgiving before God?

The psalm declares that God's faithfulness continues 'to all generations.' How does trusting God's faithfulness across generations shape the way you think about your own children, grandchildren, or those who will come after you in the faith?
Jesus calls Himself the good Shepherd who knows His sheep by name. How does it change your sense of belonging to know that the Maker of all the earth also knows you personally through Christ?

translationNotes.note: A well known textual variant renders this phrase 'and not we ourselves' rather than 'and we are His,' both readings emphasizing that human beings owe their existence entirely to God rather than to themselves.
The Hebrew word rendered 'joyful noise' (rua) is the same root used elsewhere for a victory shout, indicating exuberant, triumphant praise rather than quiet reflection.

'Loving devotion' renders the Hebrew chesed, God's steadfast covenant love, paired here with 'faithfulness,' emunah, a word elsewhere used of firm, load-bearing pillars, underscoring God's utter reliability.

meta.notes: The local scripture toolkit returned a disk I/O error in this sandbox, so the BSB text was obtained via WebSearch and web_fetch from biblehub.com/bsb/psalms/100.htm, cross-checked against the section map's 5-verse bound. Footnote markers were stripped; no em or en dashes were present in the source text for this psalm. The superscription 'A Psalm of thanksgiving.' was folded into verse 1 as printed by the source, keeping the verse count at 5. Sermon link for Alistair Begg's 'A Psalm for Giving Thanks' was verified this session via web_fetch of truthforlife.org, which lists the sermon's scripture keyword as Psalm 100:1-5.
